critic_raw:
This experiment tests a CNN for early-stage lung cancer detection against radiologist consensus sensitivity, using a stratified split of 10,000 CT scans.

The baseline is radiologist consensus sensitivity on the same test set. The CNN's decision threshold is selected on the validation set 'to match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity.' This means the threshold is chosen to match a specific sensitivity value, and the reported test sensitivity will be at or above that value by construction (since any threshold achieving higher sensitivity is acceptable). The real comparison is sensitivity at matched specificity or at matched operating point. The design does not report specificity at the chosen threshold on the test set, nor does it verify that the CNN achieves the same sensitivity with comparable or better specificity. A CNN that achieves radiologist-equivalent sensitivity by flagging 90% of all scans as positive would pass this criterion while being clinically useless.

Multiple CNN architectures are evaluated on the validation set (ResNet-50 with ImageNet pretraining, a custom 3D volumetric CNN, or a Vision Transformer) and the best performer is selected. This constitutes implicit multiple comparisons over architectures. The selected architecture will have higher expected validation performance than any individual model, inflating the apparent performance advantage on the test set. No correction for this model selection step is applied.

The label is defined through radiologist consensus plus confirmatory histopathology or 24-month follow-up for negatives. For the negative cases confirmed by 24-month clinical follow-up (rather than biopsy), there is an implicit assumption that lung cancer cannot develop within 24 months from the scan date and go undetected. This is not a safe assumption for very early-stage cancer: a scan from month 1 may show a small nodule that develops into cancer confirmed by month 20, which would be correctly labeled positive. However, a scan from month 20 of the follow-up period would have only 4 months of follow-up at the time of label assignment, making 24-month confirmation incomplete for recently acquired scans. The design does not specify how recent scans at the end of the 5-year archive are handled.

The 3D volumetric CNN and the ResNet-50 (2D) architectures process fundamentally different input representations: one operates on full 3D CT volumes, the other on 2D slices. If the architecture selection picks the 3D CNN, it has access to volumetric context (nodule shape in 3D) that the 2D ResNet does not. The architectures are not comparable in terms of information access, so selecting between them by validation performance conflates architectural choice with information access choice — again an uncontrolled degree of freedom.
all_issues_raised: The CNN's threshold is selected to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity by design, but the corresponding test specificity is not constrained. The comparison does not establish that the CNN achieves equivalent sensitivity at comparable or better specificity — a model flagging all scans as positive trivially passes.

Three qualitatively different architectures (ResNet-50 2D, 3D volumetric CNN, Vision Transformer) are evaluated and the best selected. This is an uncontrolled model selection step that inflates expected test performance through implicit multiple comparisons, without correction.

Negative labels confirmed by 24-month follow-up are incomplete for scans acquired in the final 24 months of the 5-year archive. These scans may have received 'negative' labels on insufficient follow-up, contaminating the test set with false negatives.

The candidate architectures differ in input dimensionality (2D slices vs. 3D volumes), making the architecture selection an implicit choice between different information access modalities, not just different architectural inductive biases.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN's threshold is selected to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity by design, but the corresponding test specificity is not constrained. The comparison does not establish that the CNN achieves equivalent sensitivity at comparable or better specificity — a model flagging all scans as positive trivially passes.

Three qualitatively different architectures (ResNet-50 2D, 3D volumetric CNN, Vision Transformer) are evaluated and the best selected. This is an uncontrolled model selection step that inflates expected test performance through implicit multiple comparisons, without correction.

Negative labels confirmed by 24-month follow-up are incomplete for scans acquired in the final 24 months of the 5-year archive. These scans may have received 'negative' labels on insufficient follow-up, contaminating the test set with false negatives.

The candidate architectures differ in input dimensionality (2D slices vs. 3D volumes), making the architecture selection an implicit choice between different information access modalities, not just different architectural inductive biases.
